query: What are the Five Pillars of Islam?
response: The Five Pillars of Islam form the foundation of a Muslim's faith and practice. They include the declaration of faith, or ["Shahada"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Baha'i/Core%20Tablets/the-bab_bismillahir-rahmanir-rahim_ar-4#p1), which is the testimony that there is no god but Allah and Muhammad is His messenger. The second pillar is ["Salat"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Baha'i/Core%20Tablets/the-bab_kitabur-ruh_ar#p403), the performance of ritual prayers five times a day. The third is ["Zakat"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Baha'i/Core%20Tablets/the-bab_kitabur-ruh_ar#p403), or almsgiving, which involves giving to those in need. The fourth pillar is ["Sawm"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Baha'i/Core%20Tablets/the-bab_bismillahir-rahmanir-rahim_ar-4#p1), fasting during the month of Ramadan. Lastly, the fifth pillar is the ["Hajj"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Baha'i/Core%20Tablets/the-bab_bismillahir-rahmanir-rahim_ar-4#p1), the pilgrimage to Mecca, which every Muslim must undertake at least once in their lifetime if they are able. These practices are essential to the faith and help to cultivate a sense of community and spiritual discipline.

query: How do Buddhism and Hinduism differ on the concept of self?
response:
In Buddhism, the concept of self is often seen as an illusion. The Buddha taught that what we consider the "self" is actually a collection of changing elements, and thus, there is no permanent, unchanging self. This is encapsulated in the teaching of ["anatta"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Buddhist/Pali%20Canon/tr-nanamoli-thera_majjhima-nikaya-9-sammaditthi-sutta-the-discourse-on-right-view#p16), or "non-self," which suggests that clinging to the idea of a permanent self leads to suffering.

In contrast, Hinduism generally holds the belief in an eternal soul, or ["Atman"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Baha'i/Core%20Tablets/the-bab_compilation-from-point-of-bayan_ar#p564), which is considered the true self and is identical with Brahman, the ultimate reality. This belief in a permanent self is central to many Hindu philosophies, where realizing the unity of Atman and Brahman is seen as the path to liberation. Thus, while Buddhism denies a permanent self, Hinduism affirms it as the essence of one's being.

Bahá'u'lláh advises to ["abandon not the everlasting beauty for a beauty that must die"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Baha'i/Tablet%20Translations/bahaullah_parallel-hidden-words-in-english-early-and-authorised#p255), urging a focus on eternal rather than transient attractions. Similarly, in the *Four Valleys*, he speaks of the wayfarer who will never reach their spiritual homeland without being ["empty of what is in the hands of the people"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Baha'i/Tablet%20Translations/bahaullah_chahar-vadi-four-valleys#p30), highlighting the importance of spiritual over material wealth. These teachings encourage a shift from the temporary allure of the physical world to the enduring values of the spiritual realm.

query: Why does God allow suffering?
response: The question of why God allows suffering is a profound one that many traditions have grappled with. In Buddhism, the [Four Noble Truths](https://siftersearch.com/library/Buddhist/Pali%20Canon/tr-sister-vajira_digha-nikaya-16-mahaparinibbana-sutta-last-days-of-the-buddha#p113) teach that suffering is an inherent part of life, arising from desire and attachment, and that liberation is possible through the Eightfold Path. Bahá'u'lláh reflects on the trials faced by the faithful, noting that they ["sacrificed their homes and property"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Baha'i/Tablet%20Translations/bahaullah-abdul-baha_tablets-of-bahaullah-and-abdul-baha-from-star-of-the-west#p54) as a testament to their devotion. This suggests that suffering can be a means to demonstrate faith and resilience. In Christianity, Jesus' words in the Gospel of Luke remind us to consider our own lives and actions, as he tells the ["Daughters of Jerusalem, weep not for me"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-luke/?paraId=para_845), indicating a focus on personal reflection and transformation. Each tradition offers a unique perspective on the role of suffering in the human experience.

query: Do all religions lead to the same truth?
response: The question of whether all religions lead to the same truth is a profound one, and perspectives vary across traditions. The Báb suggests a unity in diversity, stating that ["the paths to God are as numerous as the breaths of the creatures"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Baha'i/Tablet%20Translations/the-bab_the-spiritual-journey-towards-god#p7), yet ultimately there is "no religion but the one religion." This implies a fundamental unity beneath apparent differences. Similarly, 'Abdu'l-Bahá emphasizes that ["the foundations of the religions of God are one foundation"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Baha'i/Core%20Talks/abdul-baha_christians-muhammadans-and-jews#p12), suggesting that the core truths are shared, even if the expressions differ. These views invite us to see beyond the surface and recognize a shared spiritual reality.

query: What is progressive revelation, and which texts in the library discuss it?
response: Progressive revelation is the concept that religious truth is revealed progressively and cyclically through a series of divine messengers, each building upon the teachings of their predecessors. Bahá'u'lláh emphasizes this by stating that ["the Revelation sent down by God hath most surely been repeated"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Baha'i/Tablet%20Translations/bahaullah_surat-al-bayan-the-surah-of-the-exposition#p25), indicating a continuity and renewal of divine guidance. This idea is further supported by the notion that ["the outstretched Hand of Our power hath overshadowed all"](https://siftersearch.com/library/Baha'i/Tablet%20Translations/bahaullah_surat-al-bayan-the-surah-of-the-exposition#p25), suggesting an ongoing divine influence throughout history. The library contains texts like Bahá'u'lláh's *Surat al-Bayán* that delve into this theme, illustrating how each revelation is part of a larger, unfolding divine plan.

query: Progressive revelation is just Bahá'u'lláh saying all religions are basically the same?
response: Actually, progressive revelation in the Bahá'í Faith isn't about saying all religions are the same, but rather that ["religious truth is not absolute but relative"](https://oceanlibrary.com/call-to-the-nations_shoghi-effendi/?paraId=para_10), as Shoghi Effendi explains. This means that divine revelation is a continuous and progressive process, where each religion builds upon the previous ones. Bahá'u'lláh describes the prophets as ["the suns of His names and attributes"](https://oceanlibrary.com/commentary-on-surah-of-sun_bahaullah/?paraId=para_27), each enlightening their respective nations. Thus, while the core principles are harmonious, each revelation is suited to the needs of its time and people.
